title: SRE & Observability for SAP
description: Observability is the foundation of site reliability engineering. This module covers SLI/SLO design for SAP workloads (background jobs, RFC, Fiori, OData), error budget policies, OpenTelemetry integration for ABAP, central logging architecture from Syslog to Elasticsearch/Splunk, alert fatigue reduction strategies, and incident response with postmortem culture. Staff+ engineers build observable systems, not just monitored ones.

description.value:
Service Level Indicators (SLIs) and Service Level Objectives (SLOs) are the foundation of Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) as practiced at Google and adopted across FAANG. For SAP landscapes, defining meaningful SLIs requires understanding the unique characteristics of SAP workloads — batch jobs with completion deadlines, synchronous RFC calls, long-running Fiori transactions, and mixed real-time and near-real-time data flows. An SLO for SAP is not simply uptime; it must capture the user experience of completing a business process.

SLI Categories for SAP: (1) Availability SLI: The fraction of time the SAP system is reachable and accepts requests. Measured via synthetic probing (transaction SMON, or an external system pinging the ICM port). The key distinction is system-level availability (the AS ABAP is up) vs service-level availability (the Fiori launchpad renders, the RFC destination responds). Service-level availability is what matters to users. (2) Latency SLI: The response time for key user journeys — Fiori app initial load (<1000ms target), OData query (p95 <500ms), RFC call (<200ms), BAPI commit (<2000ms). Latency must be measured from the user's network location, not from the application server internal metrics. This requires real user monitoring (RUM) agents in the browser or synthetic monitoring from multiple geographic regions. (3) Throughput SLI: The number of successfully completed business processes per minute — purchase orders created, sales orders shipped, invoices posted. This reflects the system's actual business value delivery. (4) Freshness SLI: For analytical/reporting systems — how current is the data? Measured as the time between a database change and its availability in reporting views. For CDS-based Fiori analytical apps, this is the replication or delta-queue latency.

SLO Design Patterns: SLOs should be set at a granularity that reflects business impact. A monolithic SLO of 99.9% uptime for the entire SAP landscape is meaningless because it hides subsystem degradation. Instead, define SLOs per critical business process: Order-to-Cash (OData + RFC + batch) should have a separate SLO from Record-to-Report (BW + extraction). The SLO target should be based on the error budget calculation — not aspirational but achievable within the existing system's performance characteristics. SLOs must have measurement windows: a rolling 28-day window (standard SRE practice) detects trends, while a 7-day window detects regressions. The FAANG pattern is to use multiple windows: the compliance window (28 days, for reporting) and the burn rate window (1 hour, for alerting).

Staff+ Insight: SLI definition for SAP requires deep understanding of business processes, not just system metrics. An SLI for Order-to-Cash must account for: the Fiori UI latency, the OData service response, the BAPI commit duration, and the background job that posts the invoice. Missing any component gives a false sense of reliability. Design SLIs from the user's perspective — what does a successful transaction look like to the business user?

* SLI measurement: Fiori app load time via custom monitoring
* Transaction: ST03N (Workload monitor)
* Use SMON for synthetic probing

CLASS zcl_sli_reporter DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: report_odata_latency
      IMPORTING iv_service TYPE string
                iv_duration TYPE i
                iv_success  TYPE abap_bool,
      flush_to_splunk.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mt_slis TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zsli_entry.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_sli_reporter IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD report_odata_latency.
    DATA(ls_entry) = VALUE zsli_entry(
      sli_type    = 'latency'
      sli_name    = 'odata_' && iv_service
      timestamp   = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
      duration_ms = iv_duration
      success     = iv_success
      system_id   = sy-sysid
      client      = sy-mandt ).
    APPEND ls_entry TO mt_slis.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD flush_to_splunk.
    IF mt_slis IS INITIAL.
      RETURN.
    ENDIF.
    " Send to Syslog (RFC 5424) or HTTP to Splunk HEC
    " Use cl_http_client for Splunk HTTP Event Collector
    " Batch: 500 entries per request
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* SLO compliance check (run weekly via background job)
DATA(lv_28day_uptime) = ... " compute from SLI entries
DATA(lv_slo_target) = 0.999.

IF lv_28day_uptime < lv_slo_target.
  DATA(lv_budget_remaining) = 1 - ( 1 - lv_28day_uptime )
    / ( 1 - lv_slo_target ).
  " If budget_remaining < threshold -> alert, freeze deployments
  " FAANG pattern: error budget policy drives deployment velocity
ENDIF.

decisionTree.title: Choosing Which SLIs to Monitor First
decisionTree.prompt: With limited observability budget, which SLIs should I implement first?
decisionTree.firstQuestion: "Which user journey causes the most business pain when it fails?" If the financial close process is frequently delayed, instrument that. If Fiori app users complain about slowness, instrument that. Start with the highest-business-impact user journey, not the easiest technical metric.

branches.condition: Batch jobs frequently miss deadlines (month-end close, payroll)
branches.path: Instrument batch job SLIs first: completion rate, duration vs schedule, failure count. These are the easiest to instrument (SM37 logs) and have the highest business impact. Set up burn-rate alerts for jobs running past their window.

branches.condition: Fiori / OData latency degrading user experience
branches.path: Instrument frontend SLIs: Fiori app load time, OData query latency (p50/p95/p99), error rate per app. Use SAP Cloud ALM or custom RUM. Target: Fiori p95 < 1s, OData p95 < 500ms.

Instrument integration SLIs: RFC success rate, IDoc processing time, error rate by partner. Use SAP PI/PO or Event Mesh monitoring. Target: RFC success rate > 99.9%, IDoc processing within SLA window.

decisionTree.staffPlusTip: Never start with infrastructure SLIs (CPU, memory, disk). They correlate poorly with user experience. Start with business-process-level SLIs (order creation success rate, batch completion rate, report generation time). Infrastructure SLIs are debugging tools, not service-level indicators.

description.value:
The error budget is the core SRE contract between development and operations teams. The error budget for a service is simply 1 minus SLO (e.g., a 99.9% SLO gives a 0.1% error budget, or ~43 minutes of downtime per month). As long as the service stays within its error budget, releases proceed at the normal velocity. When the error budget is exhausted, releases are paused — stability takes priority over features. This creates a shared accountability model: if the operations team wants higher reliability, they negotiate a tighter SLO (smaller error budget). If development wants faster releases, they accept a lower SLO (larger error budget).

Burn Rate Alerting: The standard SRE approach for alerting based on error budgets is burn rate monitoring. Instead of paging on every error spike, you calculate how fast the error budget is being consumed — the burn rate. A burn rate of 1 means the error budget lasts the full window (e.g., 1 month). A burn rate of 2 means it lasts half the window. A burn rate of 10 means it lasts 3 days. The multi-window burn rate approach uses two windows: a short window (1 hour, burn rate >= 14) for immediate page, and a longer window (6 hours, burn rate >= 6) for warning page. This eliminates the noise of transient spikes while catching persistent degradation.

Error Budget Policy in SAP Context: For SAP landscapes, the error budget policy must account for planned downtime (maintenance windows, quarterly upgrade stacks, SAP Security Patch Day). The SLO measurement window should exclude planned downtime — otherwise, teams are penalized for activities that reduce future risk. The error budget policy also defines the escalation path: at 50% budget consumed, the SRE team investigates; at 80%, a postmortem is triggered; at 100%, all non-critical changes are blocked. This policy must be configured in the deployment pipeline — for ABAP, this means gating transport imports through SAP Solution Manager's Change Request Management (ChaRM) or the BTP CI/CD service. The AWS pattern (CodePipeline with approval gates) and the Google pattern (Spinnaker with deployment conditions) both support this — BTP's CI/CD service should be configured to reject transports when the error budget is exhausted.

* Error budget monitoring in ABAP
* Run via background job (Z_ERROR_BUDGET_CHECK) every hour

DATA lv_slo_target TYPE decfloat16 VALUE '0.999'.
DATA lv_window_start TYPE utclong.
DATA lv_window_end   TYPE utclong.

" 28-day compliance window
lv_window_start = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( ) - 28.
lv_window_end   = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( ).

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM zsli_entry
  WHERE timestamp BETWEEN @lv_window_start AND @lv_window_end
  INTO @DATA(lv_total).

SELECT COUNT(*) FROM zsli_entry
  WHERE success = @abap_false
    AND timestamp BETWEEN @lv_window_start AND @lv_window_end
  INTO @DATA(lv_bad).

IF lv_total > 0.
  DATA(lv_actual_uptime) = 1 - ( lv_bad / lv_total ).
  DATA(lv_budget_remaining) =
    ( lv_actual_uptime - ( lv_slo_target - ( 1 - lv_slo_target ) ) )
    / ( 1 - lv_slo_target ).

  IF lv_budget_remaining < 0.2.
    " CRITICAL: page SRE, freeze transports, trigger postmortem
  ELSEIF lv_budget_remaining < 0.5.
    " WARNING: log incident, schedule investigation
  ENDIF.
ENDIF.

* 1-hour burn rate for fast detection
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM zsli_entry
  WHERE timestamp > @cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( ) - 1/24
  INTO @lv_total.
SELECT COUNT(*) ... INTO @lv_bad ...
DATA(lv_1h_bad_rate) = lv_bad / ( lv_total / ( 30 * 24 * 60 ) ).
* If lv_1h_bad_rate >= 14 -> page immediately

cp.monthlyPenalty: Tight SLO (99.99%): 4.3 min/month error budget. Cost: must freeze releases for 3 weeks before quarter-end to preserve budget. Each release risk = 2 min of potential downtime. Max: 2 releases/month.

ap.monthlyPenalty: Relaxed SLO (99.9%): 43 min/month error budget. Cost: slightly lower availability target. Benefit: 10x more release capacity. Each release consumes same 2 min risk. Max: 21 releases/month.

calculation.breakeven: For SAP landscapes, a 99.9% SLO with monthly releases costs 30% less than 99.99% with quarterly releases, while delivering features 3x faster. Only tighten to 99.99% for P1 financial close windows or regulatory reporting periods.

description.value:
Distributed tracing in SAP landscapes is challenging because a single business transaction (e.g., a purchase order creation) can traverse: Fiori UI — BTP Gateway — OData service — ABAP function module — RFC — on-premise ECC — IDoc — middleware — cloud storage. Without distributed tracing, understanding the latency and error contribution of each component is impossible. OpenTelemetry (OTel) is emerging as the standard for instrumenting these heterogeneous flows, and SAP is increasingly adopting OTel for BTP services and ABAP Cloud.

OpenTelemetry Concepts: OpenTelemetry defines three pillars: (1) Traces — end-to-end transaction paths composed of spans. Each span represents a unit of work (e.g., an ABAP method, an RFC call, a database query). Spans carry trace IDs, parent span IDs, timestamps, attributes, and status. (2) Metrics — aggregated measurements (request count, latency histogram, error rate) that can be tagged with dimensions. (3) Logs — structured log records with severity, timestamp, and attributes. The OpenTelemetry protocol (OTLP) exports these telemetry data to a backend (Jaeger, Zipkin, Grafana Tempo, or Datadog). The W3C Trace Context standard (traceparent header) is used for trace propagation across services.

ABAP OTel Implementation: SAP ABAP does not yet have a native OpenTelemetry SDK, but you can instrument ABAP using: (1) Custom OTel exporter in ABAP: Use cl_http_client to send OTLP/gRPC or OTLP/HTTP protobuf payloads to the OTel collector. The collector acts as an agent that receives telemetry from all systems and forwards to the backend. (2) ABAP call stack interception: Use ABAP's GET RUN TIME and GET TIME to create span timing data. For RFC tracing, the SM59 transaction can log RFC call durations, but this is not automatically correlated with the upstream trace — you need to propagate the trace context (trace ID, span ID) via RFC extension headers or via BTP's sap-trace-id header. (3) BTP services: BTP's Application Runtime (Cloud Foundry) supports OTel natively. The BTP Gateway (XSUAA) propagates trace context via sap-passport headers, which can be converted to W3C Trace Context format for interoperability with non-SAP systems.

Staff+ Architecture: The architecture requires an OTel Collector per SAP datacenter (to handle buffering, sampling, and filtering before forwarding to the central backend), trace context propagation through all middleware (Cloud Connector, SAP PO/PI, message queues), and a tail-based sampling strategy (record all errors, sample 10% of successful traces). This reduces storage costs while preserving debugging information for failures. For high-volume SAP systems (100k+ RFC calls/hour), the instrumentation overhead must be less than 3% to avoid impacting production latency.

x="350" y="355">Sampling: 100% errors + 10% successful traces</text><text class="sys-lbl" x="350" y="370">Instrumentation overhead target: <3% added latency</text></svg>

* ABAP OTel trace span generation pattern
* SAP does not provide a native OTel SDK for ABAP (as of 2025)

CLASS zcl_otel_trace DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    METHODS: start_span
      IMPORTING
        iv_trace_id  TYPE string
        iv_span_id   TYPE string
        iv_parent_id TYPE string OPTIONAL
        iv_name      TYPE string
      RETURNING
        VALUE(rv_span_id) TYPE string,
      end_span
        IMPORTING iv_span_id TYPE string
                  iv_error   TYPE abap_bool OPTIONAL.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
    DATA mt_spans TYPE STANDARD TABLE OF zotel_span.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_otel_trace IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD start_span.
    rv_span_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c36_static( ).
    APPEND VALUE #(
      trace_id    = iv_trace_id
      span_id     = rv_span_id
      parent_id   = iv_parent_id
      span_name   = iv_name
      start_time  = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
    ) TO mt_spans.
  ENDMETHOD.

  METHOD end_span.
    DATA(ls_span) = mt_spans[ span_id = iv_span_id ].
    ls_span-end_time = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( ).
    ls_span-status = COND #(
      WHEN iv_error = abap_true THEN 'ERROR' ELSE 'OK' ).
    MODIFY mt_spans FROM ls_span TRANSPORTING end_time status
      WHERE span_id = iv_span_id.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* W3C Trace Context format: traceparent
* 00-{trace_id}-{span_id}-{flags}
* Example: 00-0af7651916cd43dd8448eb211c80319c-b7ad6b7169203331-01

* Export via OTLP HTTP to Collector
* POST /v1/traces (protobuf binary)
* Use cl_http_client to build and send protobuf payload
* For performance: batch spans, flush every 10s or per 1000 spans

organizationalDimension.title: Adopting OpenTelemetry Across an SAP Enterprise
organizationalDimension.migrationStrategy: The 'Trace-First' approach: Phase 1 (months 1-3) deploys an OTel collector and instruments the top 5 highest-traffic OData services with custom span generation. Phase 2 (months 4-6) extends tracing to RFC calls by wrapping BAPI/FM calls with span propagation. Phase 3 (months 7-9) integrates IDoc processing and batch jobs into the trace model. Phase 4 (months 10-12) builds dashboards and alerts based on trace-derived metrics.

stakeholders.concern: OTel collector adds network overhead and requires infrastructure we do not currently run
stakeholders.address: The OTel collector runs as a sidecar on existing SAP app servers (no new VMs). Network overhead is ~2% of total traffic based on FAANG benchmarks. Start with a single collector instance for 5 services.

stakeholders.concern: Instrumenting existing ABAP code with tracing spans is high effort with no immediate benefit
stakeholders.address: Phase 1 only instruments 5 services with ~50 lines of ABAP code per service. The immediate benefit: when a slow Fiori app is reported, you can see exactly which backend call is slow. No more 'it's not my service' debates.

stakeholders.concern: We already have SAP Solution Manager monitoring — why add another tool?
stakeholders.address: SolMan monitors system health (is the system up?). OTel monitors transaction health (is the purchase order flow fast?). They are complementary. SolMan pages when the system is down. OTel traces show WHY it is slow.

organizationalDimension.adoptionPhases: Phase 1: 5 OData services instrumented. Phase 2: RFC span propagation. Phase 3: IDoc + batch tracing. Phase 4: Trace-derived dashboards and alerts.
organizationalDimension.metrics: Month 3: 5 services traced, trace data flowing to OTel backend. Month 6: 20 services traced, MTTR for OData latency issues reduced by 40%. Month 12: 80% of critical paths traced, MTTR reduced by 60%.
organizationalDimension.antiPatterns: (1) Trying to instrument everything at once — focus on critical paths. (2) No sampling strategy — at 10M+ spans/day, storage costs explode. Use head-based sampling (10% for lower environments, 1% for production). (3) No correlation with existing logs — traces without logs miss context.

sections.title: Central Logging Architecture — Syslog to Elastic / Splunk

description.value:
Central logging is the foundation of observability — you cannot instrument what you cannot see. In SAP landscapes, logs are generated by the ABAP application server (syslog, dev_access, dev_w0, security audit log), HANA database (trace files, audit log), ICM (HTTP access log, error log), and SAP Start Service (sapstart log). Each log source has its own format, rotation policy, and retention. A central logging architecture consolidates these heterogeneous logs into a single queryable platform (Elasticsearch, Splunk, Azure Log Analytics, or Google Cloud Logging) with unified schema, correlation IDs, and retention policies.

Syslog Ingestion Strategy: SAP's default logging writes to flat files in /usr/sap/<SID>/<instance>/work/. The standard pattern is to configure rsyslog or syslog-ng on the SAP application server to tail these files and forward them. For each log file, define: (1) the file path and rotation pattern, (2) the syslog facility and severity mapping (e.g., ABAP application log to local0.info, security audit log to local1.warning), (3) the log format transformation (SAP multiline logs must be aggregated into single events). For HANA, use the HANA stats server or the syslog_export setting in global.ini to forward trace logs to syslog. The ICM log is forwarded via icm/security_log to syslog or directly to a socket.

Log Format and Schema: Every log event should be normalized to a common schema: timestamp (UTC), severity, source system (SID), component (ABAP, HANA, ICM), host, instance number, user (if available), transaction/program name, correlation ID, and the raw message. The correlation ID is the most critical field — it enables stitching logs across components. SAP's sap-passport can serve as the correlation ID. If sap-passport is not available, generate a UUID per request at the API gateway and propagate it via HTTP headers or RFC extension parameters. Structured logging (JSON format) is strongly preferred over unstructured text — Elasticsearch and Splunk perform significantly better with structured data because they can index fields natively without guessing patterns.

Log Retention and Tiering: A retention policy balances storage cost against investigation needs. Standard practice: (1) Hot tier (Elasticsearch fast nodes or Splunk indexes) — 7 to 30 days for interactive querying. (2) Warm tier (Elasticsearch warm nodes or Splunk frozen) — 90 to 365 days for occasional querying. (3) Cold tier (S3/GCS/Azure Blob) — 1 to 7 years for compliance. Logs containing personal data (GDPR) must be automatically pseudonymized or excluded from the cold tier. The retention policy must be automated — manual cleanup fails under the log volume of an enterprise SAP landscape (hundreds of GB per day).

* ABAP: Send structured log to syslog (RFC 5424 format)

DATA lo_syslog TYPE REF TO cl_abap_syslog.
DATA lv_msg_id TYPE string.

lo_syslog = cl_abap_syslog=>create(
  iv_appl_log = 'Z'
  iv_log_type = 'CUS' ).

lv_msg_id = cl_system_uuid=>create_uuid_c36_static( ).

* Structured log as JSON for better Elasticsearch indexing
DATA(lv_structured) = |{{"timestamp":"|
  && cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
  && |","sid":"{ sy-sysid }"|
  && |,"user":"{ sy-uname }"|
  && |,"program":"{ sy-cprog }"|
  && |,"message":"Order { lv_order_id } created",|
  && |"order_id":"{ lv_order_id }"|
  && |,"amount":{ lv_amount }|
  && |,"correlation_id":"{ lv_trace_id }"|
  && |}}|.

CALL FUNCTION 'Z_SEND_SYSLOG'
  EXPORTING
    iv_severity = 'INFO'
    iv_msg      = lv_structured
    iv_facility = 'local0'.

* For Splunk HEC:
* POST /services/collector/event HTTP/1.1
* Authorization: Splunk <hec_token>
* {"event": {...structured log fields...}}

* Elasticsearch ILM policy:
* PUT _ilm/policy/sap_log_policy
* {
*   "policy": {
*     "phases": {
*       "hot":  { "actions": { "rollover": { "max_size": "50GB" }}},
*       "warm": { "min_age": "30d", "actions": { "migrate": {} }},
*       "cold": { "min_age": "90d", "actions": { "freeze": {} }},
*       "delete": { "min_age": "365d", "actions": { "delete": {} }}
*     }
*   }
* }

Alert fatigue is the single biggest operational problem in enterprise SAP landscapes. When every batch job failure, every long-running RFC, and every disk space warning pages the same SRE team, the team learns to ignore alerts — and misses the one alert that matters. The solution is not to add more alerts but to redesign the alerting system around SRE principles: fewer alerts, higher signal-to-noise ratio, and clear actionability criteria.

Alert Classification: Every alert must be classified as: Page (immediate human response required — system down, data corruption, security breach), Ticket (respond within 1 business day — disk at 80%, certificate expiring in 2 weeks), or Log (no action required — informational). The goal is to eliminate middle-of-the-night pages for non-urgent issues. The guideline: if the alert does not require someone to wake up and start working immediately, it should not page. Implement this via Prometheus Alertmanager severity routing or your monitoring tool's equivalent. For SAP Solution Manager, this means configuring the alert rule severity correctly and routing pages through PagerDuty only for severity 1 (outage) and severity 2 (degradation). Severity 3+ goes to the ticket queue.

Runbook and TTD/TTR: Every pageable alert must have a runbook — a step-by-step guide for handling the alert. The runbook specifies: (1) symptoms (what the alert looks like), (2) triage (how to determine severity and affected users), (3) immediate actions (restart service, kill job, activate fallback), (4) escalation criteria (when to involve the development team). The Time to Detect (TTD) is the time between the incident start and the first alert. The Time to Repair (TTR) is the time from alert to verified recovery. Both must be measured and tracked. At FAANG, the SRE team operates to a TTD target of less than 5 minutes for critical services and TTR of less than 30 minutes. For SAP landscapes, these targets must be adjusted — a TTR of less than 1 hour for a batch failure is reasonable, but less than 5 minutes for a Fiori app outage is expected.

Incident Command System (ICS): For major incidents, the incident response follows the Incident Command System: an Incident Commander (IC) coordinates the response, SMEs diagnose the issue, and a scribe documents the timeline. The IC is not the most senior person — it is the person currently on-call who has been trained to coordinate. The IC's role is to manage communication, not to fix the issue. This structure prevents the common failure mode of all senior engineers debugging simultaneously without coordination. After resolution, the postmortem is written within 48 hours — blame-free, focused on system failures, with action items that are tracked to completion.

* Alert classification dispatch
CLASS zcl_alert_dispatch DEFINITION.
  PUBLIC SECTION.
    TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_alert,
             severity  TYPE i, " 1=Page, 2=Ticket, 3=Log
             message   TYPE string,
             runbook   TYPE string,
             component TYPE string,
           END OF ty_alert.
    METHODS dispatch IMPORTING is_alert TYPE ty_alert.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_alert_dispatch IMPLEMENTATION.
  METHOD dispatch.
    CASE is_alert-severity.
      WHEN 1.
        " Page via PagerDuty / Opsgenie / ServiceNow
        " CALL FUNCTION 'Z_PAGERDUTY_TRIGGER'
      WHEN 2.
        " Create ticket in ServiceNow / Jira
        " CALL FUNCTION 'Z_CREATE_INCIDENT'
      WHEN 3.
        cl_abap_syslog=>create( )->log(
          iv_message = is_alert-message ).
    ENDCASE.
  ENDMETHOD.
ENDCLASS.

* Incident timeline recording
INSERT INTO zincident_timeline VALUES (
  incident_id  = lv_incident_id
  timestamp    = cl_abap_utcl=>get_system_time( )
  event_type   = 'DETECTED'
  description  = |Batch job Z_PAYROLL failed - timeout|
  actor        = 'SYSTEM'
  duration_sec = 0 ).

* Later events: ASSIGNED, TRIAGE_START, DIAGNOSIS,
* MITIGATION, RESOLVED, POSTMORTEM

* Postmortem tracking
* Store in Z table: title, date, severity, root_cause, action_items
* Action items must have owners and due dates
* Measured: TTD, TTR, action item completion rate

Postmortem culture is the differentiator between organizations that improve reliability and those that repeat the same incidents. At FAANG companies, postmortems are blame-free by design — the assumption is that the system failed, not the person. The postmortem's goal is to identify system-level improvements (automation, better monitoring, architectural changes) that prevent recurrence, not to assign blame. SAP landscapes, with their complex inter-system dependencies and legacy code, benefit enormously from this culture — but implementing it requires organizational change beyond technical tools.

Postmortem Structure: Every postmortem follows a standard template: (1) Incident Summary: one-paragraph description of what happened, when, and impact. (2) Timeline: chronological sequence of events with timestamps, including detection, notification, diagnosis, mitigation, and resolution. (3) Root Cause Analysis (RCA): the underlying cause, distinguished from trigger (the immediate event). For example: trigger = batch job aborted due to memory limit; root cause = the job's memory requirement grew by 30% over 6 months without being reprofiled. (4) Contributing Factors: anything that made the incident worse — missing alert, insufficient capacity, stale runbook. (5) Action Items: specific, measurable, owner-assigned tasks with deadlines. Each action item is categorized as: prevent recurrence, improve detection, reduce impact, or accelerate recovery. (6) Lessons Learned: what the team learned about the system or process.

Postmortem Cadence and Metrics: Postmortems should be written for every incident that meets the severity threshold (customer-visible impact or error budget consumption exceeding 10%). The postmortem must be written within 48 hours of resolution — memories fade, and the system logs may be recycled. The SRE team tracks postmortem quality metrics: (1) Action item completion rate — are improvements being implemented? Target: greater than 90% completion within 90 days. (2) MTTR (Mean Time to Repair) trend — are incidents being resolved faster? (3) Incident recurrence — are the same types of incidents happening repeatedly? If yes, the action items were insufficient. (4) Postmortem velocity — are postmortems being written within 48 hours? (5) Blame-free adherence — as measured by incident reviews and peer feedback.

Staff+ Role: As a Staff+ engineer, you model the postmortem culture by writing exemplary postmortems for incidents in your area, participating in postmortem reviews without blame language, and advocating for the action items in sprint planning. You also design the error budget-driven improvement backlog: when the error budget is consumed, the team's capacity shifts from feature development to reliability improvements — the postmortem action items are the highest-priority items in that backlog.

* Postmortem data model (Z_POSTMORTEM table)
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_postmortem,
         incident_id   TYPE string,
         title         TYPE string,
         severity      TYPE i,
         incident_date TYPE d,
         trigger       TYPE string,
         root_cause    TYPE string,
         contributing  TYPE string,
         summary       TYPE string,
         timeline      TYPE string,  " JSON array
         actions       TYPE string,  " JSON array
         lessons       TYPE string,
         status        TYPE c LENGTH 1, " D=draft, R=reviewed, C=closed
       END OF ty_postmortem.

* Postmortem action item
TYPES: BEGIN OF ty_action_item,
         action_id    TYPE string,
         incident_id  TYPE string,
         description  TYPE string,
         category     TYPE c LENGTH 1,
           " P=prevent, D=detect, M=mitigate, R=recover
         owner        TYPE uname,
         due_date     TYPE d,
         status       TYPE c LENGTH 1, " O=open, I=in-progress, C=closed
         completed_on TYPE d,
       END OF ty_action_item.

* Quality metrics query: action item completion rate
SELECT COUNT(*) AS total,
       SUM( CASE WHEN status = 'C' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END ) AS completed
  FROM zpostmortem_actions
  WHERE due_date BETWEEN @lv_start AND @lv_end
  INTO @DATA(ls_metrics).

DATA(lv_completion_rate) = ls_metrics-completed / ls_metrics-total.
IF lv_completion_rate < 0.9.
  " Escalate to engineering leadership — action items overdue
ENDIF.

* Blame-free language check (postmortem review guideline):
* BAD: "Engineer X deployed without testing"
* GOOD: "The deployment pipeline did not include automated tests"
* Focus on system failures, not individual mistakes

adversarialResponse.title: Handling 'Blameless postmortems do not work in practice. People still get blamed.'
adversarialResponse.challenge: "You propose blameless postmortems, but in my experience, the person who caused the incident always gets blamed, regardless of the stated culture. This is naive."
adversarialResponse.badResponse: "Blameless culture requires leadership buy-in. If your leadership does not support it, that is the problem." (defensive, shifts responsibility away from the speaker)
adversarialResponse.goodResponse: "You are right that declaring 'blameless culture' is not enough — it requires systemic reinforcement. Here are the specific mechanisms I use: (1) Incident Commander role: the most junior engineer on-call is never the IC — the IC is a designated senior who owns the timeline and communication, removing the pressure from the person who made the error. (2) Postmortem template: the root cause field requires a SYSTEM cause, not a PERSON cause. If the root cause says 'engineer X ran wrong command,' the postmortem is rejected and returned for rewrite — 'no runbook for the procedure' or 'production access not gated by approval workflow.' (3) Action items are tracked as system improvements, not personal remediation. (4) Leadership is trained: if a VP asks 'who is responsible' in a postmortem review, the SRE director redirects to 'what system failure allowed this to happen.' The last mechanism is the most important — leadership behavior sets the culture. I have seen blameless culture work at companies where VPs were trained to ask about system failures, not individual mistakes."
adversarialResponse.pattern: specific-mechanisms-not-vibes — do not defend the concept abstractly. Name specific mechanisms (IC role, template enforcement, action item tracking, leadership training) that make blameless culture real. The Staff+ signal is knowing that culture is not declared — it is engineered through process.

details: Staff+ Insight: Observability and SRE are the most transferable skills between FAANG and SAP. The SLI/SLO framework, error budget policies, and postmortem culture are identical across all large-scale systems — the only difference is the SAP-specific instrumentation. Demonstrating mastery of SRE principles in an SAP context proves you understand reliability at a platform level, not just within a single codebase. Google's SRE book is the canonical reference; being able to map every chapter to an SAP equivalent is a powerful interview signal.
